parentQuestion: A tissue consists of groups of similar cells that have a common function.

scaffolds.q: The body is organized hierarchically: cells → tissues → organs.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Each level builds on the one below it.

scaffolds.q: A TISSUE is the level between cells and organs.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Cells group together to form tissues.

scaffolds.q: Tissues are made of SIMILAR cells - cells of the same type.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Muscle tissue contains muscle cells. Nerve tissue contains nerve cells.

scaffolds.q: The cells in a tissue work together toward a COMMON FUNCTION.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Muscle tissue contracts. Nerve tissue transmits signals.

scaffolds.q: There are FOUR primary tissue types: epithelial, connective, muscle, nervous.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Every tissue in your body fits into one of these four categories.

scaffolds.q: Epithelial tissue covers and lines surfaces.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Skin surface, gut lining, blood vessel lining - all epithelium.

scaffolds.q: Definition: Tissue = similar cells + common function.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This is the precise definition of a tissue.

scaffolds.q: The statement correctly defines tissue: similar cells, common function.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Both parts of the definition are accurate.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - tissues are groups of similar cells with common function.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This is the standard definition of tissue.
